CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Jun-18-2018 Off Calendar

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Apr-30-2018 Off Calendar

MANDATORY SETTLEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-30-2018 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Jul-03-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Apr-02-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Mark S. Askanas, Esq. and Amelia L. Sanchez-Moran, Esq. of Jackson Lewis, PC, T:415.394.9400, for defendant/moving party;
Daniel Feder, Esq. of the Law Offices of Daniel Feder, T:415.391.9476, for plaintiff/opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT KH PSF, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication, as follows:

First (Disability Discrimination: Gov't Code 12940): DENIED. Plaintiff asserts he was terminated discriminatorily and without notice after a month's leave for a severe hematoma. A medical leave can be a reasonable accommodation, though plaintiff is wholly unable to work during the leave. (Nunes v. Wal-Mart Stores, Inc. (9th Cir. 1999) 164 F.3d 1243, 1247.) This and the evidence that defendant did no cost-benefit analysis of hiring someone to replace plaintiff temporarily (Kueffner Dep. 19-20, Cox Dep. 22-23), shows triable issues of fact. Second (Failure to Prevent Harassment, Discrimination and/or Retaliation: Gov't Code 12940): DENIED. The triable issues as to the first cause of action also preclude summary adjudication here. Third (Retaliation: Gov't Code 12940): DENIED. A request for a reasonable accommodation can be a protected activity for a retaliation claim. The opinion defendant relies upon was superseded January 1, 2016 by Government Code 12940 (l)(4), (m)(2). Fourth (Labor Code 1102.5): GRANTED. The complaint pled that plaintiff "refused to participate in discriminatory and harassing conduct prevalent in Defendant's workplace," but plaintiff adduced no evidence of that. Fifth & Sixth (Wrongful Discharge in Violation of Public Policy/Gov't Code 12940): DENIED. The triable issues addressed above preclude summary adjudication of these causes of action. Seventh (Failure to Provide Reasonable Accommodations: Gov't Code 12940): DENIED. Disputes of material fact exist as to whether (1) plaintiff requested "indefinite" leave, (2) light-duty work was ever discussed and/or (3) a temporary replacement was considered. (Kueffner Dep. 14-15, 17, 19-20; Holmer Dec. paras. 17, 20, 22, 25.) The opinions defendants cites are inapposite; they regard leaves of absence much longer than plaintiff's one month. Eighth (Failure to Engage in Interactive Process: Gov't Code 12940): DENIED. A reasonable trier of fact could find that defendant's termination of plaintiff "without advance warning or further discussion was unreasonable and caused a breakdown in the interactive process." (See Nadaf-Rahrov v. Neiman Marcus Group, Inc. (2008) 166 Cal.App.4th 952, 989.) Ninth (Sick Leave Ordinance): GRANTED. It is undisputed that plaintiff was allowed to use all his accrued sick leave.

ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: G. Gonzales; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR#6576, email: rhunter@sftc.org.
